passage.title: What Water Will Hold
passage.body_md:
The *solubility* of a salt in water is the maximum mass of the salt that will dissolve in a given volume of water at a given temperature. A solution that contains this maximum mass of dissolved salt is said to be *saturated*; any salt added beyond this amount remains as undissolved solid.

Students measured the solubility of three salts—Salt A, Salt B, and Salt C—in water at six temperatures between 0°C and 100°C. For each trial, the students added a salt to 100 mL of water held at a fixed temperature, stirred until no additional salt would dissolve, and recorded the total mass of salt that had dissolved. The results are shown in Table 1.

**Table 1.** Solubility (grams of salt per 100 mL of water)

| Temperature (°C) | Salt A | Salt B | Salt C |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 13 | 35 | 21 |
| 20 | 32 | 36 | 17 |
| 40 | 62 | 37 | 13 |
| 60 | 108 | 38 | 10 |
| 80 | 167 | 39 | 7 |
| 100 | 245 | 40 | 5 |

Each solubility value in Table 1 is the average of three trials conducted at the same temperature with the same salt.

questions.stem_md: According to Table 1, the solubility of Salt A in water at 60°C is:
questions.explanation_md: In Table 1, the row for 60°C lists 108 g per 100 mL under Salt A. The value 62 g is Salt A's solubility at 40°C, 167 g is its solubility at 80°C, and 245 g is its solubility at 100°C, so those choices come from reading the wrong row.

questions.stem_md: Based on Table 1, as water temperature increased from 0°C to 100°C, the solubility of which salt(s) *decreased*?
questions.explanation_md: Salt C's solubility fell steadily from 21 g per 100 mL at 0°C to 5 g per 100 mL at 100°C, so it is the only salt whose solubility decreased. Salt A's solubility increased sharply (13 g to 245 g) and Salt B's increased slightly (35 g to 40 g), so any choice that includes Salt A or Salt B describes an increasing trend, not a decreasing one.

questions.stem_md: According to Table 1, the solubilities of Salt A and Salt B in water were most likely equal at some temperature:
questions.explanation_md: At 20°C, Salt A's solubility (32 g per 100 mL) was less than Salt B's (36 g per 100 mL), but at 40°C, Salt A's solubility (62 g) was greater than Salt B's (37 g). Because Salt A's value went from below to above Salt B's between those two temperatures, the two solubilities must have been equal somewhere between 20°C and 40°C. In each of the other intervals, Salt A's solubility was either below Salt B's at both endpoints or above it at both endpoints.

questions.stem_md: Based on Table 1, the solubility of Salt A in water at 50°C was most likely closest to which of the following?
questions.explanation_md: A temperature of 50°C lies halfway between 40°C and 60°C, so Salt A's solubility at 50°C should fall between 62 g and 108 g per 100 mL; 85 g is the only choice in that range. The values 62 g and 108 g are the tabulated solubilities at 40°C and 60°C themselves, and 48 g lies below the entire interval.

questions.stem_md: A student claimed, "For any salt, increasing the temperature of the water increases the maximum mass of the salt that can dissolve." Do the data in Table 1 support this claim?
choices: Yes, because the solubility of all three salts increased as temperature increased.
Yes, because the solubility of Salt A more than doubled between 40°C and 80°C.
No, because the solubility of Salt B remained the same at all six temperatures.
No, because the solubility of Salt C decreased as temperature increased.
questions.explanation_md: The claim says heating always increases solubility, but Salt C's solubility dropped from 21 g to 5 g per 100 mL as temperature rose, so the claim is contradicted by the data. The first choice is factually wrong because not all three salts showed an increase. The second choice cites a true fact about Salt A, but one salt's increase cannot prove a claim about *any* salt. The third choice is factually wrong because Salt B's solubility rose from 35 g to 40 g rather than staying the same.

questions.stem_md: A saturated solution of Salt C was prepared in 100 mL of water at 0°C. Based on Table 1, if this solution were then heated to 100°C, approximately how many grams of Salt C would come out of solution as solid?
questions.explanation_md: A saturated solution at 0°C contains 21 g of Salt C per 100 mL. At 100°C the water can hold only 5 g per 100 mL, so 21 g − 5 g = 16 g of Salt C would come out of solution as solid. Choosing 5 g gives the mass that *remains* dissolved, 21 g is the original dissolved mass, and 26 g results from adding the two values instead of subtracting.

questions.stem_md: A chemist added 150 g of Salt A to 100 mL of water at 40°C and stirred until no additional salt dissolved, leaving some solid at the bottom of the container. Based on Table 1, if the mixture were then heated to 80°C and stirred, would all 150 g of Salt A most likely dissolve?
choices: Yes, because the solubility of Salt A at 80°C is greater than 150 g per 100 mL.
Yes, because heating a mixture always dissolves all of the solid present.
No, because the solubility of Salt A at 80°C is less than 150 g per 100 mL.
No, because a salt cannot dissolve after it has settled out of solution.
questions.explanation_md: At 40°C only 62 g of Salt A can dissolve in 100 mL, which is why solid remained, but at 80°C the solubility rises to 167 g per 100 mL—more than the 150 g added—so all of the salt would dissolve. The second choice gives the right outcome for a wrong, overly general reason (heating did not help Salt C, for example). The third choice misreads the table, since 167 g is greater than 150 g, and the fourth choice contradicts the definition of solubility, which depends on temperature rather than on whether solid has settled.
